ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific wholemeal cracker entry; closest match is the general cracker baked product class FOODON_03301710.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers crackers and crispbreads as a category; no specific wholemeal cracker sub-code identified in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for wholemeal cracker; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of wholemeal crackers is approximately 4 crackers (25 g), providing around 133 kcal, 6.7 g fat, 15.6 g carbohydrate, and 111 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal Cracker — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wholemeal cracker (cr_id F11080082). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous whole wheat cracker entry (FDC ID 172023). Extended micronutrient fields not reported by HPB for this record are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Crackers and Crispbreads — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'wholemeal cracker' was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: whole wheat crackers / crispbreads (GI range 64–69, mean ~67) as published in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × available carbohydrate per serving (15.6 g) / 100 = 10.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 444 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit wholemeal cracker intake to no more than 2–3 crackers per sitting and choose low-sodium variants (ideally <120 mg sodium per 100 g) to avoid exceeding the daily sodium target of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (62.2 g/100 g) partially offset by dietary fibre (4.4 g/100 g) and medium GI (~67)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portions to 2–3 crackers (approximately 25 g) per serving to limit carbohydrate load to ~15.6 g per serving, and pair with a protein or healthy fat source to further blunt the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (26.7 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~5.5 g/100 g and possible trans fat from partially hydrogenated oils in commercial formulations
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should check the ingredient label for partially hydrogenated vegetable oils (a source of trans fat) and select wholemeal cracker brands certified trans-fat free with saturated fat below 5 g per 100 g; sodium at 444 mg/100 g also warrants portion control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potential presence of trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils used in commercial cracker production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid commercial wholemeal crackers that list partially hydrogenated oils in the ingredient declaration, as even trace trans fat is strongly pro-inflammatory; choose brands explicitly certified trans-fat free.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal cracker is a baked, crisp, dry snack made primarily from whole grain wheat flour, vegetable oil, salt, and water. The use of wholemeal (whole grain) flour retains the bran and germ layers of the wheat kernel, contributing dietary fibre (4.4 g per 100 g) and a denser, slightly nutty flavour compared to refined wheat crackers. The texture is firm and crunchy with a mild savoury taste. Nutritionally, wholemeal crackers are energy-dense at 533 kcal per 100 g, driven by a high fat content of 26.7 g per 100 g and a high carbohydrate content of 62.2 g per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 444 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~67) and moderate fibre content make them a marginally better choice than refined crackers for blood glucose management, but carbohydrate load per serving remains significant. Wholemeal crackers are widely consumed as a snack across Singapore and globally, often paired with cheese, nut butter, or dips. They are suitable for vegetarians and vegans and are typically halal-certified. Individuals with wheat or gluten allergies must avoid this product. Clinical caution is warranted for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high carbohydrate, medium GI), cardiovascular disease (fat quality), and rheumatoid arthritis (potential trans fat from commercial processing).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a wholemeal cracker?
Is wholemeal cracker good for weight loss?
How much sodium is in a wholemeal cracker?
Is wholemeal cracker suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of wholemeal crackers?
Wholemeal cracker vs refined cracker nutrition
How many crackers is one serving?
meal_context: paired with cheese or nut butter

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with exact match to cr_id F11080082. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous whole wheat cracker entry (FDC ID 172023). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous whole wheat cracker/crispbread range. All other micronutrient fields are set to -1 where neither HPB nor a secondary approved source reports them for this specific product.
notes: Saturated fat (5.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for whole wheat crackers (USDA FDC ID 172023) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this record. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the HPB Foodo database does not report these values for cr_id F11080082, and no secondary approved source was used to supplement them in order to avoid undocumented mixing of source provenance: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields were confirmed as not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this food item as of the accessed date 2026-04-16. Trans fat presence in commercial wholemeal crackers is a known risk from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils; however, trans_fat_g is set to -1 as no approved source provides a confirmed numeric value for this specific generic product — the HPB Foodo database does not report trans fat for this entry, and USDA FDC SR Legacy entry for whole wheat crackers does not provide a reliable trans fat figure applicable to this Singapore HPB record. This food is a generic Western snack product with no mandatory multilingual naming requirement under Project Veritas v2 rules (multilingual requirements apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes); local_names fields are therefore null. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database whole wheat cracker/crispbread range (GI 64–69); no direct entry for 'wholemeal cracker' was found.
